iec104.label: IEC 60870-5-104
iec104.note: Suricata has a parser; rule renderers deferred to v2.

iec61850-mms.label: IEC 61850 MMS
iec61850-mms.note: Shares port 102 with S7Comm; no Suricata parser. Cover with Zeek icsnpp-mms.

bacnet.label: BACnet/IP
bacnet.note: No Suricata parser. Cover with Zeek icsnpp-bacnet.

opcua.label: OPC UA Binary
opcua.note: Suricata has a dissector since 6.0; rule renderers deferred to v2.

hart-ip.label: HART-IP
hart-ip.note: Niche protocol. Flag if asset claims this role; no built-in coverage.

detections.title: Modbus write_single_coil (FC5) from non-HMI source
comment: An HMI writes a coil to set a status indicator. Any other source
writing coils means an engineer poking from a non-approved host or
someone walking through write codes to see what is exposed.

detections.title: Modbus write_single_register (FC6) from non-HMI source
comment: Setpoint write to a holding register. Of all standard Modbus writes
this is the most often used to feed bad data into a process.

detections.title: Modbus write_multiple_coils (FC15) from non-HMI source
comment: Bulk coil write. Same intent as FC5 across a range. Used during
display refreshes by some HMIs but never from a non-HMI source.

detections.title: Modbus write_multiple_registers (FC16) from non-HMI source
comment: Bulk register write, the largest single-shot impact in standard
Modbus. Push a whole block of attacker-chosen values in one PDU.

detections.title: Modbus mask_write_register (FC22) observed
comment: Bitwise modify of a holding register. Almost never seen in normal
HMI traffic; common in attack scripts that flip a single control
bit without disturbing surrounding state.

detections.title: Modbus read_write_multiple_registers (FC23) from non-HMI source
comment: FC23 atomically reads one register range and writes another in
the same PDU. Functionally a write from a detection standpoint:
the write half lands before the read response returns.

detections.title: Modbus diagnostics (FC8) from non-engineering source
comment: FC8 sub-function 4 is Force Listen Only Mode, an attacker DoS
primitive. Suricata cannot parse the sub-function so we alert on
the function broadly and let the analyst pivot.

detections.title: Modbus encapsulated_interface_transport (FC43)
comment: FC43 carries device identification. Asset-scanning tools and
attackers both use it for vendor and firmware fingerprinting.

detections.title: DNP3 write (FC2) from non-master source
comment: DNP3 writes only originate from documented masters. From elsewhere
this is an unauthorized master, a misconfigured device, or an
attacker positioned to issue parameter changes to the RTU.

detections.title: DNP3 select (FC3) from non-master source
comment: First half of select-before-operate. Almost always pairs with FC4
from the same source seconds later. Correlate by source IP.

detections.title: DNP3 operate (FC4) from non-master source
comment: The message that actually moves the breaker, opens the valve, or
whatever the control point does. Commits the prior FC3 select.

detections.title: DNP3 direct_operate (FC5) from non-master source
comment: Direct operate skips the select handshake. More dangerous than
FC3+FC4 because the control happens in one PDU.

detections.title: DNP3 cold_restart (FC13) of RTU
comment: Cold restart forces the outstation to reboot. No legitimate reason
outside a planned maintenance window. Page the on-call engineer.

detections.title: DNP3 warm_restart (FC14) of RTU
comment: Warm restart is softer than FC13 but the outstation still drops
service. Same conclusion: page, verify maintenance window.

detections.title: DNP3 stop_application (FC18) of RTU
comment: Stops the outstation software while the device responds to ICMP.
Preferable to a restart from an attacker's perspective.

detections.title: DNP3 open_file (FC25) from non-engineering source
comment: DNP3 file ops move configuration and firmware. Open file starts
a config push or pull.

detections.title: DNP3 activate_config (FC31) from non-engineering source
comment: The moment a previously pushed config takes effect. Coupled with
FC25 it is the full configuration-replacement sequence.

detections.title: CIP Set_Attribute_Single (0x10) from non-engineering source
comment: Most common single-attribute write in CIP. Should originate from
engineering. PLC-to-PLC peer messaging is allowlisted via the
plc_peer role.

detections.title: CIP Set_Attributes_All (0x02) from non-engineering source
comment: Replaces an entire object's attribute set in one operation.
Higher impact than 0x10 because changes are atomic.

detections.title: CIP Logix Write Tag (0x4D) from non-engineering source
comment: AB-specific service for direct controller tag writes. The most
operationally significant write on a running Logix PLC.

detections.title: CIP Write Tag Fragmented (0x53) from non-engineering source
comment: Multi-PDU tag write for values larger than a single CIP packet.
Used for arrays and structures. From outside engineering this
is an attacker pushing structured data into running logic.

detections.title: CIP Read Tag (0x4C) from non-engineering source
comment: Single-tag read. HMIs poll tags constantly so this is allowlisted
for HMI as well as engineering. Off by default to avoid noise;
enable in environments where HMI tag access is tightly scoped.

detections.title: CIP Read Tag Fragmented (0x52) from non-engineering source
comment: Multi-PDU read for tags larger than one packet. Same allowlist
as 0x4C; off by default for the same reason.

detections.title: CIP Get Instance Attributes List (0x55) from non-engineering source
comment: Tag-browse service: enumerates tag names and types in the
controller. Engineering tools (RSLogix, Studio 5000) use this
during connection. Anything else is asset discovery.

detections.title: CIP Execute PCCC (0x4B) from non-engineering source
comment: Legacy bridge: tunnels PA-PC PCCC commands inside CIP for
compatibility with PLC-5/SLC-class devices. Often used by
older HMIs and asset-discovery tooling.

detections.title: CIP Reset (0x05) of PLC
comment: CIP Reset reboots the controller. Almost never legitimate over
the network. Critical regardless of source.

detections.title: CIP Stop (0x07) of PLC
comment: Transitions controller out of run mode. Stuxnet-class operation:
stop, modify, restart. Page on first occurrence.

detections.title: S7Comm Read Var (0x04) from non-engineering or non-HMI source
comment: Tag-value read. HMIs poll continuously so this is allowlisted for
HMI and engineering. Off by default to avoid noise; enable in
environments where Read Var traffic should be tightly scoped.

detections.title: S7Comm Write Var (0x05) from non-HMI source
comment: Tag-value write to an S7-300/400. Should originate only from
documented HMIs. Look at the DB and offset in the payload.

detections.title: S7Comm Request Download (0x1A) from non-engineering source
comment: First message of a program-download sequence. From outside
engineering this is the start of program replacement.

detections.title: S7Comm Download Ended (0x1C) from non-engineering source
comment: Commit message of the download sequence. After this fires the
new program is resident on the PLC. Critical.

detections.title: S7Comm Start Upload (0x1D) from non-engineering source
comment: Program extraction direction. An attacker pulling logic down to
study, modify, replay. Often precedes a Stuxnet-style swap.

detections.title: S7Comm PLC Stop (0x29)
comment: Halts CPU execution. Stuxnet's exact operation: freeze the PLC
while modifications happen. Page on every occurrence.

detections.title: S7Comm PLC Hot Start (0x28)
comment: Resumes execution after a stop. A 0x28 from the same source as
a recent 0x29 is the smoking-gun signature of program
modification.

detections.title: GE-SRTP Write System Memory (0x07) from non-HMI source
comment: Service code 0x07 writes to PLC system memory: registers, analog
outputs, discrete outputs. The SRTP equivalent of S7 Write Var
or Modbus FC6/16.

detections.title: GE-SRTP Write Program Memory (0x09) from non-engineering source
comment: Service 0x09 writes to program memory. Modifies the running
logic on the PLC, equivalent to S7 program download. Critical.

detections.title: GE-SRTP Write Task Memory (0x08) from non-engineering source
comment: Service 0x08 writes to task memory. Less impactful than 0x09
but still a configuration change. Engineering only.

detections.title: GE-SRTP Read Program Memory (0x06) from non-engineering source
comment: Service 0x06 reads program memory. The exfiltration direction:
downloading the PLC program for offline study. Often precedes
a 0x09 program-write attack.

detections.title: GE-SRTP Privilege Login (0x80) from non-engineering source
comment: Privilege login elevates the session to write-capable state.
SRTP requires this for writes. From outside engineering this
IS the start of an attack.

detections.title: GE-SRTP Return Controller Type (0x43)
comment: Service 0x43 returns the controller's vendor/model string.
Recon equivalent of Modbus FC43.
